enrichment.short_description: AI recruiting search engine that uses natural-language queries (PeopleGPT) to find and engage candidates across 800M+ profiles.
enrichment.seo_title: Juicebox Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Juicebox (PeopleGPT) review 2026: AI-powered recruiting search, pricing tiers, sourcing agents, ATS integrations, pros, cons and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Juicebox, also widely known by its flagship product name PeopleGPT, is an AI-powered talent sourcing and recruiting search engine founded in 2022 by David Paffenholz (CEO, a Harvard economics graduate who previously worked on growth at Snap) and Ishan Gupta (a former BlueLearn software engineer who left Dartmouth College to build the company). The company launched through Y Combinator's Summer 2022 (S22) batch and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Juicebox's core innovation is replacing traditional Boolean search strings used by recruiters with natural-language queries: a recruiter types a plain-English description of the ideal candidate, and Juicebox's large-language-model-powered engine translates that into a structured search across a database of more than 800 million professional profiles aggregated from public web and professional network data. Beyond search, Juicebox offers always-on AI sourcing Agents that run in the background, learn from a recruiter's approvals and rejections, continuously refine search criteria, and deliver fresh shortlists without manual re-searching; this Agent capability is sold as a $199-per-agent-per-month add-on on top of any paid plan. The platform also provides automated, personalized outreach sequencing, contact-data enrichment (email and phone numbers unlocked via contact credits), and deep integrations with applicant tracking systems including Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workday, BambooHR, SmartRecruiters, iCIMS, JobAdder, Personio, Oracle Taleo and dozens more. Juicebox has raised a total of roughly $36 million in earlier rounds, including a $30 million Series A led by Sequoia Capital in September 2025 with participation from Y Combinator, and in March 2026 it closed an $80 million Series B led by DST Global (with Sequoia, Coatue, Y Combinator, NFDG and Verified Capital participating) at an $850 million valuation, reporting that annual recurring revenue had tripled since the Series A and that it now serves roughly 5,000 customers ranging from early-stage startups to Fortune 100 companies and leading AI labs (publicly cited users include Ramp and Perplexity). Pricing is structured as a free trial with limited searches, a Starter plan at $139 per seat per month, a Growth plan at $199 per seat per month, and a custom-priced Business plan for larger teams; annual billing brings the effective price down by roughly 15 percent, which is the source of marketing copy advertising plans 'from $119'. All paid plans include unlimited searches and unlimited shortlisted candidates per project, with contact credits metered separately for revealing verified emails and phone numbers. Juicebox targets in-house talent acquisition teams, recruiting agencies, and hiring managers at technology companies who want to source passive candidates faster than manual Boolean search on LinkedIn Recruiter allows. Its most direct competitors are LinkedIn Recruiter (the incumbent, Boolean-search-based sourcing tool), HeroHunt.ai, SeekOut, Fetcher, and other AI-native sourcing tools like hireEZ; Juicebox differentiates primarily through its conversational natural-language search interface, its always-on autonomous Agents that proactively resurface candidates, and its rapid enterprise traction validated by top-tier venture investors within roughly three years of founding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Juicebox is an AI-native recruiting platform built around PeopleGPT, a natural-language search engine that lets recruiters describe a candidate in plain English instead of constructing Boolean search strings. Founded in San Francisco in 2022 by David Paffenholz and Ishan Gupta and launched out of Y Combinator, the company has grown quickly, closing an $80 million Series B at an $850 million valuation in March 2026 after tripling annual recurring revenue in under a year.

The platform searches across more than 800 million public professional profiles and returns ranked, relevant matches based on the intent behind a query rather than exact keyword matches. This makes it useful for sourcing passive candidates whose profiles do not use the exact job titles or skill terms a recruiter would otherwise search for.

Juicebox has expanded from a pure search tool into a fuller sourcing workflow product, adding autonomous AI Agents, outreach sequencing, contact enrichment, and native ATS integrations so that recruiters can move from search to outreach without leaving the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The centerpiece feature is natural-language candidate search: recruiters type a free-text description of role requirements, seniority, skills, and location, and the underlying LLM converts that into a structured query executed against Juicebox's profile index, returning ranked candidates with explanations of match relevance.

AI sourcing Agents extend this by running continuously in the background rather than on demand. An Agent learns from which candidates a recruiter approves or rejects, adapts its criteria over time, and delivers fresh candidate batches automatically, which is positioned as a way to keep a sourcing pipeline warm without recurring manual searches.

Rounding out the platform, Juicebox offers contact-credit-based email and phone enrichment, personalized outreach sequencing, and two-way integrations with major applicant tracking systems (Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workday, SmartRecruiters, iCIMS and dozens of others), letting sourced candidates flow directly into a recruiting team's existing pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Juicebox uses a seat-based subscription model with a free trial offering a limited number of searches. Paid tiers are Starter at $139 per seat per month and Growth at $199 per seat per month, with a custom-quoted Business plan for larger recruiting teams needing advanced controls, dedicated support, or higher usage limits. Annual billing discounts the monthly rate by roughly 15 percent.

AI Agents are not bundled into the base seat price; they are sold as a separate add-on at $199 per agent per month on any paid plan. Contact credits used to unlock verified emails and phone numbers are also metered, so total cost scales with both the number of seats and the volume of outreach a team runs.

features.title: PeopleGPT natural-language search
features.description: Search 800M+ candidate profiles using plain-English queries instead of Boolean strings.

features.title: AI sourcing Agents
features.description: Always-on agents that learn from recruiter feedback and continuously resurface new matching candidates.

features.title: Contact enrichment
features.description: Credit-based unlocking of verified emails and phone numbers for shortlisted candidates.

features.title: Automated outreach sequencing
features.description: Personalized, multi-step candidate outreach campaigns launched directly from search results.

features.title: ATS integrations
features.description: Native two-way sync with Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workday, SmartRecruiters, iCIMS and dozens more.

features.title: Unlimited searches and shortlists
features.description: All paid plans include unlimited searches and unlimited saved candidates per project.

features.title: Team collaboration and projects
features.description: Shared projects let recruiting teams organize searches, shortlists and notes by role.

features.title: Relevance-ranked results
features.description: Candidates are ranked and explained by match relevance rather than returned as a flat keyword list.

pros: Natural-language search dramatically speeds up sourcing versus manual Boolean strings
Large 800M+ profile index covers most professional roles and industries
AI Agents keep pipelines fresh without recurring manual re-searches
Broad ATS integration coverage fits into existing recruiting workflows
Backed by major investors (Sequoia, DST Global) with fast enterprise adoption
cons: Per-seat pricing plus separate Agent and contact-credit add-ons can raise total cost quickly
No public entry-level paid tier below $139/seat/month
Business (enterprise) pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Contact data accuracy depends on third-party sourcing and can vary by region
Relatively young company (founded 2022) compared to established sourcing incumbents

faqs.question: What is Juicebox (PeopleGPT)?
faqs.answer: Juicebox is an AI recruiting platform built around PeopleGPT, a natural-language search engine that lets recruiters find candidates by describing them in plain English rather than writing Boolean search queries.

faqs.question: How much does Juicebox cost?
faqs.answer: Juicebox offers a free trial with limited searches, a Starter plan at $139 per seat per month, a Growth plan at $199 per seat per month, and a custom-priced Business plan; annual billing saves roughly 15%.

faqs.question: Who founded Juicebox and when?
faqs.answer: Juicebox was founded in 2022 by David Paffenholz and Ishan Gupta in San Francisco, launching through Y Combinator's Summer 2022 batch.

faqs.question: Does Juicebox integrate with applicant tracking systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Juicebox integrates natively with dozens of ATS platforms including Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workday, BambooHR, SmartRecruiters and iCIMS.

faqs.question: What are Juicebox AI Agents?
faqs.answer: AI Agents are always-on sourcing automations, sold as a $199-per-agent-per-month add-on, that continuously search and learn from a recruiter's approvals and rejections to keep candidate pipelines fresh.

faqs.question: How much has Juicebox raised in funding?
faqs.answer: Juicebox has raised roughly $36 million in early funding including a $30 million Sequoia-led Series A, followed by an $80 million Series B in March 2026 led by DST Global at an $850 million valuation.

use_cases.description: Fast-growing technology companies use Juicebox to source engineering and AI talent at scale with ATS-integrated workflows.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud directory platform unifying identity, device management, SSO and MFA for modern IT teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 10 users, paid plans from $9/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: JumpCloud Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: JumpCloud review 2026: cloud directory, SSO, MFA and device management platform. Compare pricing tiers, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: JumpCloud is a cloud-based directory platform, headquartered in Louisville, Colorado, that unifies identity, device, and access management for both human and non-human (service account, API, machine) identities. The company was founded in 2012 by Rajat Bhargava and Alex Fagundes, initially built as a modern, cloud-native alternative to Microsoft's on-premises Active Directory, positioning itself as an 'Open Directory Platform' that works across Windows, macOS, and Linux and integrates with cloud infrastructure such as AWS, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, and thousands of SaaS applications via SAML and SCIM. JumpCloud's core architecture centers on a cloud directory that authenticates and authorizes users, then layers on single sign-on (SSO), multi-factor authentication (MFA), mobile device management (MDM) for laptops and mobile devices, LDAP-as-a-service, RADIUS-as-a-service, a password manager, conditional access policies, and directory insights/reporting. In more recent years the company has added Zero Trust security capabilities and AI- and SaaS-management features aimed at giving IT teams visibility into shadow SaaS usage and unmanaged devices. JumpCloud has raised a total of approximately $462 million across multiple rounds, starting with a $1.2 million seed round in December 2012, a $3 million Series A in January 2014, a $4.3 million Series B in 2015, a $20 million Series C in November 2017, a $50 million Series D in May 2019, and a Series E that closed at $100 million (an initial $75 million tranche in November 2020 followed by an additional $25 million in January 2021); the company reached a $2.63 billion valuation following an October 2021 funding round led by Sapphire Ventures and BlackRock. JumpCloud filed for and later withdrew IPO plans, remaining a private company with reported ARR in the range of roughly $200 million and around 900 employees as of 2026. Pricing is structured as a generous free tier covering up to 10 users with core identity and device management features, an a la carte model where individual features (Device Management, SSO, Cloud Directory, MFA, User Lifecycle Management, Password Manager, Cloud LDAP/RADIUS, Conditional Access) can be purchased individually starting around $3-6/user/month when billed annually, and a Device Management package at $9/user/month, an SSO package at $11/user/month, and a combined Device Identity Management package at $13/user/month, alongside bundled Platform Essentials (capped at 300 users), Platform, and Platform Prime tiers priced via custom quote for organizations wanting a fully unified identity, device and security stack. JumpCloud primarily targets IT administrators and MSPs at small and mid-sized businesses that want to avoid the cost and complexity of on-premises Active Directory or a fragmented stack of point identity tools. Its most direct competitors are Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD), Okta, Google Workspace's identity layer, OneLogin, and Rippling's IT/device management module; JumpCloud differentiates through its cross-platform (Windows/Mac/Linux) directory-first approach, its granular a la carte pricing that lets smaller companies pay only for the modules they need, and its focus on unifying both human and non-human identity management under one console.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: JumpCloud is a cloud directory platform designed to replace or modernize on-premises Active Directory by centralizing identity, device, and access management in a single cloud console. Founded in 2012 and headquartered in Louisville, Colorado, the company built its platform to work natively across Windows, macOS, and Linux, and to connect to cloud infrastructure and thousands of SaaS applications rather than being tied to a single vendor ecosystem.

The platform's directory sits at the center of an IT admin's stack: it authenticates users, manages devices, and enforces access policies, while modules for SSO, MFA, LDAP, RADIUS, and mobile device management extend that directory outward to specific use cases. This modular approach lets IT teams adopt JumpCloud incrementally rather than migrating an entire identity stack at once.

JumpCloud has scaled through eight-plus years of venture funding, reaching a $2.63 billion valuation in 2021, and has grown its product to include Zero Trust security and SaaS/shadow-IT visibility features aimed at addressing the sprawl of cloud tools that small and mid-sized IT teams now have to manage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: JumpCloud's Cloud Directory Platform provides a central identity store that supports both human users and non-human identities (API keys, service accounts), with cross-platform device management (MDM) covering patch management, policy enforcement, and remote actions for Windows, macOS, and Linux endpoints.

Access management features include single sign-on to SAML and OIDC-based SaaS applications, multi-factor authentication (including push, TOTP, and hardware key support), conditional access policies based on device trust and location, and a built-in password manager for end users.

For infrastructure-level authentication, JumpCloud offers LDAP-as-a-service and RADIUS-as-a-service so legacy on-premises applications and Wi-Fi networks can authenticate against the same cloud directory, along with directory insights and reporting, Zero Trust security controls, and newer AI/SaaS management tooling that surfaces unmanaged or unsanctioned application usage across an organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: JumpCloud offers a free tier for organizations with up to 10 users that includes core identity and device management functionality, making it accessible to very small teams before they need to pay. Beyond that, pricing follows an a la carte, per-user-per-month model: individual features such as Cloud Directory, MFA, SSO, User Lifecycle Management, and Password Manager are priced separately (roughly $3-6/user/month annually), while pre-bundled packages combine common feature sets: Device Management at $9/user/month, SSO at $11/user/month, and Device Identity Management at $13/user/month (annual billing; monthly billing runs $2/user higher on each).

For organizations wanting a single unified solution rather than assembling modules, JumpCloud offers Platform Essentials (capped at 300 users), Platform, and Platform Prime bundles, all priced via custom sales quote, with Platform Prime adding Zero Trust, AI and SaaS management, and premium support. A 30-day free trial is available across the product line, and JumpCloud offers custom discounted rates for education and nonprofit organizations.

features.title: Cloud directory
features.description: Centralized identity store for human and non-human identities, replacing on-prem Active Directory.

features.title: Cross-platform device management (MDM)

features.description: SAML/OIDC-based SSO into thousands of SaaS applications from one login.

features.title: Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
features.description: Push, TOTP, and hardware-key MFA enforced at the directory level.

features.title: LDAP and RADIUS as a service
features.description: Cloud-hosted LDAP and RADIUS so legacy apps and Wi-Fi networks authenticate against the same directory.

features.title: Conditional access policies
features.description: Access rules based on device trust, location, and risk signals.

features.title: Password manager
features.description: Built-in end-user password manager included in SSO and higher packages.

features.title: Zero Trust and SaaS management
features.description: Visibility into shadow SaaS usage and Zero Trust access controls in the Platform Prime tier.

pros: Generous free tier covers up to 10 users with real identity and device features
Cross-platform support for Windows, macOS and Linux in one directory
Flexible a la carte pricing lets small teams pay only for needed modules
Unifies human and non-human identity management in one console
Reduces or eliminates need for on-premises Active Directory infrastructure
cons: A la carte pricing can become complex to configure for larger feature sets
Full Platform and Platform Prime tiers require custom sales quotes, not self-serve
Per-user-per-month cost can add up quickly for larger organizations
Some advanced SSO app integrations require higher-tier packages
Feature breadth means a learning curve for teams new to unified IT management

faqs.question: What is JumpCloud?
faqs.answer: JumpCloud is a cloud-based directory platform that centralizes identity, device, and access management, functioning as a modern, cross-platform alternative to on-premises Active Directory.

faqs.question: Is JumpCloud free?
faqs.answer: Yes, JumpCloud offers a free plan covering up to 10 users with core identity and device management features; paid plans start at $9/user/month for Device Management.

faqs.question: When was JumpCloud founded?
faqs.answer: JumpCloud was founded in 2012 and is headquartered in Louisville, Colorado.

faqs.question: Does JumpCloud replace Active Directory?
faqs.answer: JumpCloud is commonly used to replace or reduce dependence on on-premises Active Directory by moving directory services, SSO, MFA, and device management to the cloud.

faqs.question: What operating systems does JumpCloud support?
faqs.answer: JumpCloud provides native device management for Windows, macOS, and Linux endpoints.

faqs.question: How much funding has JumpCloud raised?
faqs.answer: JumpCloud has raised approximately $462 million total, reaching a $2.63 billion valuation after an October 2021 round led by Sapphire Ventures and BlackRock.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Python/Django platform for managing community-supported agriculture cooperatives and their members.
enrichment.seo_title: juntagrico Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: juntagrico review 2026: open-source Django software for CSA cooperatives covering member management, shares, deliveries and billing. Features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: juntagrico is a free, open-source software platform purpose-built to manage the administrative operations of Community-Supported Agriculture (CSA) organizations, known in German-speaking Switzerland as 'Solawi' (Solidarische Landwirtschaft). The project originated from internal tooling built for Ortoloco, a Swiss CSA cooperative, and was later generalized and rebuilt starting in 2017 so that other CSA groups could adopt it as a standalone product rather than a one-off internal tool. It is built entirely on Python and the Django web framework, with its source code hosted publicly on GitHub and developed collaboratively by contributors from participating CSA organizations. Governance and ongoing development are coordinated by the Verein Juntagrico (the Juntagrico Association), a nonprofit membership association that gives adopting CSA organizations a formal say in the software's roadmap; the association also offers free hosting of a shared juntagrico instance to its member organizations, while any organization is free to self-host the open-source codebase independently without joining the association or paying license fees. Functionally, juntagrico addresses the operational complexity specific to CSA cooperatives: it manages member registration and administration, tracks cooperative share certificates and financial deposits, administers vegetable/product subscriptions and their sizing, coordinates the mandatory co-worker (volunteer labor) obligations that many CSA models require of members, generates weekly delivery and depot (distribution point) lists, and produces the reporting needed to reconcile subscriptions against harvest capacity. Optional extensions add QR-bill invoicing (aligned to the Swiss QR-invoice payment standard) and tools for coordinating spontaneous, one-off work assignments beyond the standard volunteer schedule. As of recent counts, juntagrico is in production use at more than 25 CSA operations across Switzerland, making it a de facto standard tool in that country's CSA/Solawi movement, alongside comparable community-agriculture software such as OpenOlitor (also Swiss-developed and presented at FOSDEM). Because juntagrico is a volunteer-maintained, association-governed open-source project rather than a venture-backed company, it has no funding history, no headquarters in the corporate sense (the association and its contributor base are Switzerland-based), no pricing tiers, and no company size to report; access to the software itself is free under its open-source license, with the only recurring cost being either self-hosting infrastructure or, for association members, a membership contribution that funds shared hosting and continued development. Its target users are CSA/Solawi cooperatives, community gardens, and similar member-share agricultural organizations, typically newer or growing operations that need structured member, share, and delivery management; established operations with heavily customized processes may find they need developer involvement to adapt juntagrico's Django-based codebase to their specific workflows, since unlike commercial SaaS competitors it offers no vendor support contract by default.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: juntagrico is an open-source management platform built specifically for Community-Supported Agriculture (CSA) organizations, known as 'Solawi' in Switzerland and other German-speaking regions. It grew out of internal software originally built for the Swiss CSA cooperative Ortoloco and was rebuilt starting in 2017 so other CSA groups could adopt it independently.

The software is written in Python using the Django web framework, with its source code openly available on GitHub for self-hosting or contribution. Ongoing development is coordinated by the nonprofit Verein Juntagrico association, which gives member organizations a voice in the product roadmap and offers free shared hosting to association members.

juntagrico is now used in production by more than 25 CSA operations across Switzerland, addressing the operational needs unique to share-based agricultural cooperatives: member administration, share tracking, volunteer coordination, and weekly distribution logistics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality centers on member and subscription management: tracking who belongs to the cooperative, what share size or product subscription each member holds, and their cooperative capital deposits. This is paired with tools for coordinating the mandatory co-worker (volunteer labor) obligations that many CSA models require of members.

For distribution, juntagrico generates weekly delivery and depot (collection point) lists so organizers know exactly what quantities need to go to each distribution site, along with reporting tools to reconcile subscriptions against available harvest.

Optional extensions add Swiss QR-bill invoicing support and functionality for coordinating spontaneous, ad-hoc work assignments beyond the regular volunteer schedule, extending the base platform for organizations with more complex billing or labor coordination needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: juntagrico is free and open source under its published license; there are no paid tiers or per-user fees for the software itself. Organizations can self-host the Django application on their own infrastructure at no licensing cost, giving them full control over data and customization.

Alternatively, CSA organizations that join the Verein Juntagrico association can receive free hosting of a shared instance as part of their membership, avoiding the need to run their own servers while still gaining a say in the software's future development through the association's governance process.

features.title: QR-bill invoicing extension
features.description: Optional module for Swiss QR-invoice-compliant billing of members.

features.title: Spontaneous work assignment extension
features.description: Optional tooling for coordinating ad-hoc volunteer tasks outside the regular schedule.

features.title: Reporting
features.description: Reconciliation reports for subscriptions, deliveries, and cooperative finances.

features.title: Self-hosting on Django/Python

Purpose-built for CSA-specific workflows like share tracking and volunteer duty
Association governance gives adopting organizations input into the roadmap
Free shared hosting available to Verein Juntagrico members
Proven in production at 25+ Swiss CSA cooperatives
cons: Requires Django/Python technical knowledge for self-hosting or customization
No commercial vendor support contract by default
Primarily documented and used within the Swiss/German-speaking CSA community
Established operations with unique processes may need developer customization
Limited to CSA/cooperative use cases rather than general nonprofit management

faqs.question: What is juntagrico used for?
faqs.answer: juntagrico is open-source software used by Community-Supported Agriculture (CSA) cooperatives to manage members, product subscriptions, cooperative shares, volunteer labor coordination, and weekly delivery logistics.

faqs.question: Is juntagrico free?
faqs.answer: Yes, juntagrico is free and open source. Organizations can self-host it at no licensing cost, or join the Verein Juntagrico association for free shared hosting.

faqs.question: What technology is juntagrico built on?
faqs.answer: juntagrico is built with Python and the Django web framework, with its source code publicly available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who maintains juntagrico?
faqs.answer: The Verein Juntagrico, a nonprofit association of CSA organizations, coordinates ongoing development and governance of the software.

faqs.question: How many organizations use juntagrico?
faqs.answer: juntagrico is used in production by more than 25 Community-Supported Agriculture organizations, primarily in Switzerland.

faqs.question: Does juntagrico support invoicing?
faqs.answer: Yes, an optional extension adds support for Swiss QR-bill invoicing of cooperative members.

enrichment.short_description: AI-native, browser-based contract lifecycle management platform for creating, negotiating and managing contracts.
enrichment.seo_title: Juro Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Juro review 2026: AI-native contract management platform. Explore browser-based editing, workflow automation, pricing tiers, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Juro is a British legal technology company that builds an AI-native, browser-based contract lifecycle management (CLM) platform, founded in 2016 by Richard Mabey and Pavel Kovalevich. The company is headquartered in London, with an engineering hub in Riga, Latvia, and opened a United States headquarters in Boston, Massachusetts, in 2025 to better serve its growing North American customer base. Juro's core technical differentiator is that contracts are created, negotiated, signed, and managed entirely inside a browser-native editor rather than as Microsoft Word documents routed through email and separate e-signature tools; this browser-first architecture allows Juro to layer structured data extraction, AI-assisted drafting and review, real-time collaborative redlining, conditional approval workflows, and native e-signature directly onto the contract itself, and to expose contract terms as queryable structured data rather than locked inside static PDFs. The platform's feature set spans the full contract lifecycle: templated contract creation with dynamic clauses and conditional logic, AI-powered contract generation and clause suggestions, real-time in-browser collaboration and version control, configurable multi-step approval workflows, built-in e-signature, a searchable contract repository with AI-driven data extraction (dates, values, parties, obligations) for reporting and renewal tracking, and integrations with tools like Slack, HubSpot, Salesforce, and Workday so that non-legal teams (sales, HR, procurement) can self-serve routine contracts within guardrails set by legal. Under CEO Richard Mabey, Juro has raised roughly $36 million in disclosed venture funding from investors including Eight Roads, Union Square Ventures (USV), Point Nine Capital, and Seedcamp, along with angel backing from founders of Indeed, Gumtree, and Wise; its most notable round was a $23 million Series B led by Eight Roads Ventures in January 2022, positioned to fund international expansion and deeper AI capabilities. Pricing is not self-serve or publicly itemized per seat; Juro sells annual contracts typically ranging from roughly $15,000 to $60,000 per year depending on company size and feature tier, with its Scale and Enterprise plans offering unlimited user seats rather than per-user pricing, a deliberate positioning against legacy CLM and e-signature competitors that charge $30-80 per user per month, which can make Juro more cost-predictable for legal teams that need to give many non-legal employees contract access. Juro targets in-house legal, revenue operations, HR, and procurement teams at mid-market and enterprise companies, particularly fast-scaling SaaS and technology companies, who need to reduce contract turnaround time and give business teams safe self-serve access to contracting. Its named competitors include Ironclad, DocuSign CLM (and DocuSign's e-signature product more broadly), ContractPodAi, LinkSquares, PandaDoc, and Concord; Juro differentiates through its fully browser-native (non-Word, non-PDF) editing experience, its unlimited-seat flat-fee pricing model, and its emphasis on turning contracts into structured, queryable data rather than static signed documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Juro is a contract lifecycle management platform built around a browser-native editor rather than the traditional Word-document-plus-email-plus-e-signature workflow most legal teams use. Founded in London in 2016 by Richard Mabey and Pavel Kovalevich, the company has grown into a recognized CLM vendor with engineering based in Riga, Latvia, and a US headquarters opened in Boston in 2025 to support its expanding North American customer base.

Because contracts live natively in the browser rather than as static documents, Juro can layer AI-assisted drafting, structured data extraction, and workflow automation directly onto the contract itself, rather than bolting these capabilities onto a PDF after the fact. This lets legal teams treat contract data as a queryable, reportable asset instead of a filing cabinet of signed documents.

Juro is backed by roughly $36 million in venture funding from investors including Eight Roads, USV, Point Nine Capital, and Seedcamp, with a $23 million Series B closing in January 2022. The company positions itself against both legacy enterprise CLM suites and standalone e-signature tools, emphasizing speed of adoption and unlimited-seat pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Juro's browser-native editor supports real-time collaborative drafting and redlining, dynamic templates with conditional clause logic, and AI-assisted contract generation and review that can flag risky terms or suggest clause language during negotiation.

Workflow automation covers configurable multi-step approval chains, built-in native e-signature (no separate DocuSign integration required), and self-serve contracting flows that let non-legal teams like sales or HR generate and send routine contracts within guardrails set by the legal team.

On the data side, Juro extracts structured metadata (parties, dates, values, renewal terms, obligations) from every contract into a searchable repository, enabling reporting, renewal alerts, and integrations with systems like Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, and Workday so contract status and data flow into the tools other teams already use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Juro does not publish self-serve, per-seat pricing; instead it sells annual contracts negotiated per customer, typically ranging from roughly $15,000 to $60,000 per year depending on company size, contract volume, and feature tier. Plans are generally referred to as Scale and Enterprise, both of which include unlimited user seats rather than charging per additional user.

This flat, unlimited-seat pricing model is a deliberate contrast to competitors that charge $30-80 per user per month, since Juro's target customers often want to extend contract access broadly across sales, HR, and procurement teams without cost scaling linearly with headcount. Prospective customers request a demo and custom quote directly from Juro's sales team.

pros: Browser-native editing removes Word/PDF back-and-forth from contract negotiation
Unlimited-seat pricing avoids per-user cost scaling as adoption grows
AI-assisted drafting and data extraction speed up both creation and reporting
Strong self-serve workflows for extending contract access to non-legal teams
Backed by reputable investors with a clear focus on legal-tech innovation
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a custom sales quote
Annual contract cost ($15K-$60K/yr) can be high for very small companies
Migrating existing Word-based contract templates requires setup effort
Full value depends on broad org-wide adoption beyond the legal team
Smaller company size (51-200 employees) versus larger legacy CLM vendors

faqs.question: What is Juro used for?

faqs.question: How much does Juro cost?
faqs.answer: Juro does not publish self-serve pricing; annual contracts typically range from about $15,000 to $60,000 per year with unlimited user seats included.

faqs.question: Who founded Juro?
faqs.answer: Juro was founded in 2016 by Richard Mabey and Pavel Kovalevich in London.

faqs.question: Does Juro include e-signature?
faqs.answer: Yes, Juro includes native e-signature built into the platform, so contracts can be signed without a separate tool.

faqs.question: How is Juro different from DocuSign or Ironclad?
faqs.answer: Juro is browser-native rather than PDF- or Word-based, and uses unlimited-seat flat pricing instead of per-user fees, differentiating it from DocuSign CLM and Ironclad.

faqs.question: How much funding has Juro raised?
faqs.answer: Juro has raised approximately $36 million in total funding, including a $23 million Series B led by Eight Roads Ventures in January 2022.

enrichment.short_description: PEO platform providing payroll, benefits, HR tools and compliance support for small and mid-sized businesses.
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Justworks Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Justworks review 2026: PEO payroll, benefits and HR platform for small businesses. Compare pricing plans, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Justworks is a US-based Professional Employer Organization (PEO) and HR technology platform founded in 2012 by Isaac Oates, a former Amazon engineer, and headquartered in New York City. The company operates as a co-employer under the PEO model: it enters into a shared employment relationship with client companies' employees, which lets small and mid-sized businesses access large-group health insurance rates, simplified payroll tax administration, and compliance coverage that would otherwise be out of reach or highly complex for a small employer to manage alone. On the technology side, Justworks provides a single platform combining full-service payroll (including contractor payments), health insurance and benefits administration (medical, dental, vision, HSA/FSA, 401(k) retirement plans, and mental health/fertility benefit access), HR compliance tools, time tracking, and 24/7 customer support. In 2020, Justworks expanded beyond pure PEO services to offer Justworks Payroll, a standalone payroll-only product for companies that don't need or qualify for the PEO co-employment model, and it has since added an Employer of Record (EOR) offering to help US companies hire employees internationally without establishing a foreign legal entity. Justworks has raised multiple rounds of venture funding through a Series E and, as a private company, filed confidentially for an IPO in 2021-2022 at a reported valuation of roughly $2 billion before withdrawing those public listing plans amid volatile market conditions; it remains privately held with an employee headcount in the 1,000-5,000 range. Pricing is structured per employee per month: the PEO Basic plan costs $59 per employee per month and includes payroll, contractor payments, compliance and HR tools, and 401(k) management; the PEO Plus plan costs $109 per employee per month and adds health insurance administration, HSA/FSA account management, and mental health and fertility benefit access on top of everything in Basic; time tracking can be added to either PEO tier for an additional $8 per employee per month; and the Employer of Record product for hiring internationally is priced at $599 per employee per month, covering payroll and benefits administration for globally distributed employees. Justworks primarily targets founders and small-business operators, typically companies with roughly 2 to 500 employees, who want to outsource payroll, benefits, and compliance complexity without building an internal HR department. Its most direct competitors in the PEO space are TriNet, Insperity, Rippling PEO, Gusto, and ADP TotalSource; Justworks differentiates through its flat, transparent per-employee pricing (versus percentage-of-payroll models used by some competitors), a design-forward and simplicity-focused product experience, and strong customer support reputation among small-business operators who lack dedicated HR staff.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Justworks is a Professional Employer Organization (PEO) founded in 2012 by Isaac Oates and headquartered in New York City. As a PEO, Justworks enters into a co-employment relationship with its client companies' employees, which allows small businesses to pool into large-group health insurance plans and offload payroll tax filing and HR compliance obligations that would otherwise require significant in-house expertise.

The platform combines payroll, benefits administration, compliance support, and HR tools into a single web and mobile interface, aimed specifically at founders and operators running lean teams without a dedicated HR department. Justworks has become known for a simplified, transparent product experience relative to more complex legacy PEOs.

Beyond its core PEO offering, Justworks has expanded its product line to include Justworks Payroll (a standalone payroll product for companies that don't use the PEO model) and an Employer of Record service for hiring employees internationally, broadening its addressable market beyond traditional US-only PEO clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Justworks' core PEO service bundles full-service payroll processing (including contractor payments and tax filings), large-group health insurance access, and HR compliance guidance into one subscription, backed by 24/7 customer support for both employers and employees.

Benefits administration covers medical, dental, and vision insurance, HSA and FSA accounts, 401(k) retirement plan management, and access to mental health and fertility benefit programs, all administered through the same platform employees use for payroll and time-off requests.

Additional modules include time tracking (available as an add-on), an Employer of Record service that lets US companies legally employ workers in other countries without setting up a foreign entity, and a standalone payroll-only product (Justworks Payroll) for businesses that don't need or qualify for full PEO co-employment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Justworks prices its PEO service per employee per month across two tiers. PEO Basic costs $59 per employee per month and includes payroll, contractor payments, compliance tools, 401(k) management, and 24/7 support. PEO Plus costs $109 per employee per month and adds health insurance administration, HSA/FSA account management, and mental health and fertility benefit access on top of the Basic feature set.

Time tracking can be added to either PEO plan for an additional $8 per employee per month. For companies hiring internationally, Justworks' Employer of Record product is priced separately at $599 per employee per month, covering payroll and benefits administration for employees in other countries without requiring the client to establish a local legal entity.

faqs.question: How much does Justworks cost?
faqs.answer: Justworks PEO Basic costs $59 per employee per month and PEO Plus costs $109 per employee per month; an Employer of Record option for international hiring costs $599 per employee per month.

enrichment.short_description: All-in-one platform for creators to build, market and sell online courses, memberships and communities.
enrichment.seo_title: Kajabi Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kajabi review 2026: all-in-one course and membership platform for creators. Compare 2026 pricing tiers, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kajabi is an all-in-one creator commerce platform founded in 2010 in Irvine, California, by Kenny Rueter and Travis Rosser, built to let online course creators, coaches, and digital entrepreneurs replace a stack of separate tools (course hosting, email marketing, landing page builders, community platforms, and website hosting) with a single subscription. The platform is architected around 'products,' which can be structured as self-paced online courses, coaching offers, podcasts, communities, or digital downloads, and are sold through customizable, no-code landing pages and checkout flows, with built-in email and SMS marketing automation ('Kajabi Marketing'), automated sales funnel building ('Kajabi Pipelines'), and native community features that compete with standalone tools like Circle and Mighty Networks. Kajabi raised a $550 million funding round in May 2021 at a $2 billion valuation, led by Tiger Global with participation from TPG Capital, Tidemark Capital, Owl Rock, Meritech Capital Partners, and Spectrum Equity, making it a tech unicorn; the company has remained privately held and profitable, reportedly bootstrapped for its first decade before taking outside capital, and is headquartered in Irvine, California (sometimes cited as Newport Beach). Kajabi restructured its pricing in January 2026 for the first time since 2015, moving to four tiers: a lower-cost Starter/Basic-adjacent entry tier, Basic at $179/month billed monthly ($143/month billed annually) allowing 5 products, 1 community, 2,500 contacts, and 2 admin users; Growth at $249/month ($199/month annually) allowing 50 products, 1 community, 25,000 contacts, and 11 admin users; and Pro at $499/month ($399/month annually) allowing unlimited products, 3 communities, 100,000 contacts, and 26 admin users; all tiers include unlimited sales funnels, landing pages, marketing emails, and access groups, with Kajabi's own payment processing charged at 2.9%+$0.30 down to 2.7%+$0.30 depending on tier, and third-party payment processor fees scaling down from 5% to 0.5% as customers move up tiers. Kajabi targets online course creators, coaches, consultants, membership site operators, and content creators monetizing an audience directly, ranging from solo creators to small teams running multi-product education businesses. Its most cited competitors are Teachable, Thinkific, Podia, and, in the membership/community space specifically, Circle and Mighty Networks, along with more general-purpose funnel and marketing tools like ClickFunnels and Systeme.io; Kajabi differentiates by bundling course hosting, marketing automation, funnels, community, and payments into one platform rather than requiring creators to stitch together point solutions, positioning itself as the single operating system for a creator's entire online business rather than just a course host.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kajabi is an all-in-one platform that lets creators build, market, and sell digital products, online courses, coaching programs, memberships, communities, podcasts, and digital downloads, without needing to combine separate tools for hosting, email marketing, landing pages, and payments. Founded in 2010 in Irvine, California, by Kenny Rueter and Travis Rosser, the company grew largely through word of mouth in the creator economy before raising outside capital.

In May 2021, Kajabi raised a $550 million round led by Tiger Global at a $2 billion valuation, becoming one of the few unicorns in the creator-tools space. Despite the raise, the company has remained privately held and has continued to expand its product surface into marketing automation, community features, and AI-assisted content tools rather than pursuing acquisitions or a public listing.

The platform's core unit is the 'product', a course, community, podcast, or coaching offer, which creators build using no-code page builders, then sell through Kajabi's built-in checkout, email/SMS marketing, and automated sales funnel ('Pipeline') tools, all under a single subscription and a single customer/contact database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kajabi Products lets creators structure and host courses, coaching offers, communities, podcasts, and digital downloads using drag-and-drop, no-code builders, with customizable landing pages and checkout flows built into the same platform.

Kajabi Marketing provides email and SMS automation, audience segmentation, and broadcast campaigns, while Kajabi Pipelines offers pre-built, automated sales funnel templates (webinar funnels, product launch funnels, lead magnet funnels) so creators can go from landing page to purchase to onboarding without external funnel software.

Native community features let creators run discussion spaces and group interaction alongside their courses, competing directly with standalone community platforms, while built-in payment processing (Kajabi Payments) and analytics give creators sales and engagement reporting without a separate e-commerce or analytics integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kajabi restructured its pricing in January 2026, its first major pricing change since 2015. The current lineup includes an entry-level tier, Basic at $179/month (or $143/month billed annually) with 5 products, 1 community, 2,500 contacts, and 2 admin users, Growth at $249/month ($199/month annually) with 50 products, 1 community, 25,000 contacts, and 11 admin users, and Pro at $499/month ($399/month annually) with unlimited products, 3 communities, 100,000 contacts, and 26 admin users.

All tiers include unlimited sales funnels, landing pages, marketing emails, and access groups regardless of plan level. Payment processing fees vary by tier: Kajabi's own payment processor charges roughly 2.9%+$0.30 on lower tiers down to 2.7%+$0.30 on Pro, while transaction fees on third-party payment processors scale down from 5% on lower tiers to as low as 0.5% on Pro, incentivizing higher-volume creators to upgrade.

faqs.answer: As of Kajabi's January 2026 pricing update, Basic costs $179/month ($143/month annual), Growth costs $249/month ($199/month annual), and Pro costs $499/month ($399/month annual).

faqs.question: Who founded Kajabi?
faqs.answer: Kajabi was founded in 2010 in Irvine, California, by Kenny Rueter and Travis Rosser.

faqs.answer: Yes, Kajabi includes native community features alongside courses, though the number of communities allowed depends on the plan tier.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SIP server for building large-scale VoIP, WebRTC and real-time communication platforms.
enrichment.seo_title: Kamailio Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kamailio review 2026: open-source SIP server for VoIP and WebRTC platforms. Explore features, architecture, deployment options and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kamailio is a free, open-source SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) server used to build large-scale VoIP, WebRTC, presence, instant messaging, and real-time communication platforms, released under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2). Its history traces back to September 2001, when the project began as SIP Express Router (SER) at Fraunhofer FOKUS, a research institute in Berlin, Germany, with the codebase released as open source in September 2002, notably before RFC 3261 (SIP v2.0) itself was finalized, meaning the project's early contributors helped pioneer and shape SIP extensions alongside the standard. In 2005, a group of developers forked SER's codebase into a new project called OpenSER; that fork was renamed to Kamailio in July 2008 due to a trademark dispute over the 'OpenSER' name. Beginning in autumn 2008, the Kamailio and original SER development communities began merging their codebases, and after that merger completed, 'Kamailio' became the unified project's permanent name, chosen in part because it was better protected from future trademark conflicts. As a community-governed open-source project rather than a commercial company, Kamailio has no headquarters, employee count, funding history, or company ownership in the conventional sense; it is maintained by a distributed group of core developers and contributors worldwide, coordinated through the kamailio.org project site and its GitHub repository (github.com/kamailio/kamailio). Technically, Kamailio is built for extreme scalability and can handle thousands of call setups per second on commodity hardware, using asynchronous TCP, UDP, and SCTP transport, TLS encryption for secure signaling, WebSocket support for WebRTC integration, and dual IPv4/IPv6 support. Its feature set includes SIMPLE-based instant messaging and presence with an embedded XCAP server and MSRP relay, IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem) extensions supporting VoLTE deployments, ENUM lookups, DID (Direct Inward Dialing) routing and least-cost routing, load balancing and failover across multiple SIP endpoints, and built-in accounting, authentication, and authorization modules. It integrates with a wide range of backend data stores including MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, Radius, LDAP, Redis, Cassandra, MongoDB, and Memcached, and exposes JSON-RPC and XML-RPC control interfaces alongside SNMP monitoring for operational management. Kamailio is described by its maintainers as the oldest and among the most robust open-source SIP servers available, reportedly routing billions of VoIP minutes globally every month across telecom carriers, VoIP service providers, and enterprise communication platforms. Its target users are telecom engineers, VoIP service providers, and developers building carrier-grade or large-scale real-time communication infrastructure who need a highly configurable, high-performance SIP proxy/registrar/router rather than an off-the-shelf hosted PBX. Its closest comparables and competitors in the open-source SIP space are OpenSIPS (itself descended from the same original SER/OpenSER lineage before diverging into a separate project), FreeSWITCH (a broader open-source communication platform with a stronger media-handling focus), and Asterisk (a widely used open-source PBX); Kamailio differentiates through its specific focus on SIP signaling at very high scale, its long operating history dating to the earliest days of the SIP protocol itself, and its modular, highly extensible configuration language used by large carriers and VoIP platforms to build custom routing and signaling logic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kamailio is a free, open-source SIP server used to build large-scale VoIP, WebRTC, presence, and instant-messaging platforms. Its origins date to September 2001, when the project began as SIP Express Router (SER) at Fraunhofer FOKUS, a research institute in Berlin, meaning Kamailio's codebase predates the finalized SIP v2.0 specification (RFC 3261) and its early developers helped shape the protocol's real-world extensions.

The project went through several identity changes: a 2005 fork became OpenSER, which was renamed Kamailio in 2008 following a trademark dispute, and later that year the Kamailio and original SER communities merged their codebases into a single unified project under the Kamailio name. Today it is maintained by a distributed community of contributors rather than a single company.

Released under GPLv2, Kamailio is designed for extreme scale, capable of processing thousands of call setups per second, and is used by telecom carriers and VoIP providers worldwide to route what its maintainers describe as billions of minutes of voice traffic every month.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kamailio's networking layer supports asynchronous TCP, UDP, and SCTP transport with TLS encryption for secure signaling, dual IPv4/IPv6 support, and native WebSocket support for WebRTC-based real-time communication applications.

On the application layer, it provides SIMPLE-based instant messaging and presence with an embedded XCAP server and MSRP relay, IMS extensions for VoLTE deployments, ENUM lookups, DID and least-cost routing, and load balancing with automated failover across SIP endpoints.

Operationally, Kamailio includes built-in accounting, authentication, and authorization modules, integrates with backend systems including MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, Radius, LDAP, Redis, Cassandra, MongoDB, and Memcached, and exposes JSON-RPC/XML-RPC control interfaces plus SNMP monitoring for production operations teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kamailio is entirely free and open source under the GPLv2 license, with no paid tiers, license fees, or vendor lock-in. Anyone can download the source from GitHub, compile it, and deploy it on their own infrastructure without cost.

Because there is no company behind Kamailio, there is no official paid support tier either; organizations that need commercial support typically contract independent consultants or specialist VoIP engineering firms within the Kamailio ecosystem, or rely on community support through the project's mailing lists and IRC/chat channels.

features.title: High-performance SIP proxy/registrar
features.description: Handles thousands of call setups per second for carrier-grade VoIP routing.

features.title: WebRTC and WebSocket support
features.description: Native WebSocket transport enables browser-based real-time communication apps.

features.title: Presence and instant messaging
features.description: SIMPLE-based IM and presence with embedded XCAP server and MSRP relay.

features.title: IMS/VoLTE extensions
features.description: Supports IP Multimedia Subsystem extensions used in mobile VoLTE deployments.

features.title: Load balancing and failover
features.description: Distributes and reroutes SIP traffic automatically across available endpoints.

features.title: Least-cost and DID routing
features.description: Built-in routing logic for direct inward dialing and cost-optimized call routing.

features.title: Broad backend integration
features.description: Connects to MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis, LDAP, MongoDB, Cassandra, and more.

features.title: Monitoring and control interfaces
features.description: JSON-RPC, XML-RPC, and SNMP support for operational monitoring and management.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Proven at massive scale, routing billions of VoIP minutes monthly
Highly configurable and extensible for custom routing logic
Long operating history and mature, battle-tested codebase since 2001
Broad backend and protocol integration options
cons: Steep learning curve requiring SIP and telecom protocol expertise
No official vendor support; relies on community or third-party consultants
Configuration syntax can be complex for newcomers
Requires self-hosting and infrastructure management
Best suited to large-scale deployments, overkill for very small setups

faqs.question: What is Kamailio used for?
faqs.answer: Kamailio is an open-source SIP server used to build large-scale VoIP, WebRTC, presence, and instant-messaging platforms for telecom carriers and service providers.

faqs.question: Is Kamailio free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kamailio is completely free and open source under the GPLv2 license with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: When was Kamailio created?
faqs.answer: Kamailio's history dates to September 2001 as SIP Express Router (SER), later forked into OpenSER in 2005 and renamed Kamailio in 2008.

faqs.question: How is Kamailio different from Asterisk?
faqs.answer: Kamailio focuses on SIP signaling, proxying, and routing at very high scale, while Asterisk is a broader open-source PBX with stronger built-in media handling and call features.

faqs.question: How many calls can Kamailio handle?
faqs.answer: Kamailio is designed to process thousands of call setups per second, making it suitable for carrier-grade VoIP deployments.

faqs.question: Does Kamailio support WebRTC?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kamailio includes native WebSocket support that enables browser-based WebRTC communication applications.

use_cases.title: Carrier-grade VoIP routing
use_cases.description: Telecom carriers deploy Kamailio as a high-throughput SIP proxy and router for call traffic.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud infrastructure provider offering fully configurable, per-minute-billed VPS and IaaS hosting across global datacenters.
enrichment.headquarters: Petah Tikva, Israel (also New York, United States)
enrichment.seo_title: Kamatera Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kamatera review 2026: configurable cloud VPS and IaaS hosting from $4/month. Compare pricing plans, global datacenters, features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kamatera is a cloud infrastructure and VPS hosting provider offering fully user-configurable virtual machines billed by the minute, part of the Omnix Computers group and tracing its origins to 1995, with operational headquarters cited in both Petah Tikva, Israel, and New York, United States. The company operates roughly 18-plus datacenters spanning North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, letting customers deploy compute resources physically close to their end users for lower latency. Kamatera's core technical differentiator is granular configurability: rather than choosing from a small set of fixed instance sizes like many hyperscale clouds, customers can independently select CPU core count, RAM, storage type and size, network bandwidth, and operating system for each server, with new instances provisioned in as little as 60 seconds. The platform covers general cloud VPS/VM hosting, dedicated cloud servers, managed and unmanaged options, load balancers, firewalls, block storage, private networking, and Windows and Linux operating system support, backed by a 99.95% uptime service-level agreement covering servers, CPUs, firewalls, load balancers, and storage. Kamatera markets itself primarily on flexible, pay-for-what-you-use per-minute billing with no long-term contracts, positioning it as a cost-effective, more configurable alternative to larger hyperscale providers for developers, agencies, and SMBs running websites, applications, and dev/test environments. Pricing is structured as tiered starting configurations rather than a small number of flat plans: the current public price sheet lists roughly five base configurations ranging from about $4.00 per month at the entry tier (a small single-core VPS) up to roughly $57.60 per month for a more powerful preset configuration, with all resources fully customizable beyond these presets and total cost scaling with the exact CPU, RAM, storage, and bandwidth selected. New customers can access a 30-day free trial worth up to $100 in server usage on one server, and all paid plans include 24/7 human technical support with no additional support-tier upsell required. Kamatera targets developers, digital agencies, and small-to-mid-sized businesses that want granular control over server specifications and predictable, usage-based billing without committing to large enterprise cloud contracts, as well as managed service providers reselling infrastructure to their own clients. Its most commonly cited competitors are DigitalOcean, Linode (Akamai Connected Cloud), Vultr, and, at the larger end, AWS EC2 and Google Compute Engine; Kamatera differentiates through its highly granular per-resource configurability (rather than fixed instance tiers), its per-minute billing precision, its global datacenter footprint for low-latency deployment, and its inclusion of free 24/7 human support across all plans rather than reserving support as a paid add-on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kamatera is a cloud infrastructure provider offering fully configurable virtual private servers (VPS) and infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS), billed by the minute rather than locked into fixed monthly plans. The company traces its roots to 1995 as part of the Omnix Computers group, with operations centered in Petah Tikva, Israel, and a presence in New York, United States.

Kamatera operates more than 18 datacenters across North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, letting customers place workloads close to their end users. Its main technical differentiator versus larger hyperscale providers is granular self-service configuration: customers independently choose CPU core count, RAM, storage type and size, bandwidth, and operating system for each instance rather than picking from a small menu of fixed sizes.

New servers provision in about 60 seconds, and the platform is backed by a 99.95% uptime SLA covering compute, networking, and storage. Kamatera positions itself as a more flexible, cost-predictable alternative to both large hyperscale clouds and smaller budget VPS providers, aimed at developers, agencies, and growing businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kamatera's core offering is fully customizable cloud VPS and dedicated server instances, where CPU, RAM, storage, and network bandwidth are each independently configurable rather than bundled into fixed presets, with support for both Windows and Linux operating systems.

Infrastructure add-ons include load balancers, firewalls, block storage, and private networking, letting customers build more complex multi-server architectures directly within the platform rather than relying on third-party networking tools.

Operational features include per-minute billing precision (paying only for resources actually consumed), rapid 60-second provisioning, a 99.95% uptime SLA, and 24/7 human technical support included on all paid plans without an additional support-tier fee.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kamatera's public price sheet lists roughly five base configurations, starting at about $4.00 per month for an entry-level single-core VPS and scaling up to approximately $57.60 per month for a more powerful preset configuration with additional CPU cores, RAM, and storage. Because every resource is independently configurable, actual monthly cost scales continuously with the exact specifications a customer selects rather than being confined to these preset tiers.

All billing is calculated per minute of actual usage, so customers only pay for the time a server is running rather than a flat monthly rate regardless of usage. New customers can start with a 30-day free trial offering up to $100 in credit on one server, and every paid plan includes 24/7 human technical support with no long-term contract requirement.

features.title: Fully configurable instances
features.description: Independently select CPU cores, RAM, storage, bandwidth, and OS for each server.

features.title: Per-minute billing
features.description: Pay only for the exact minutes of compute resources actually used.

features.title: Global datacenter footprint
features.description: 18+ datacenters across North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia for low-latency deployment.

features.title: Rapid provisioning
features.description: New servers deploy and become available in about 60 seconds.

features.title: Load balancers and firewalls
features.description: Built-in networking and security infrastructure addable to any deployment.

features.title: Block storage and private networking
features.description: Attachable storage volumes and private network configuration for multi-server architectures.

features.title: 99.95% uptime SLA
features.description: Service-level guarantee covering servers, CPUs, firewalls, load balancers, and storage.

features.title: 24/7 human technical support
features.description: Included support on all paid plans without an additional support-tier fee.

pros: Highly granular configuration of CPU, RAM, storage, and bandwidth per server
Transparent per-minute billing avoids paying for unused capacity
30-day free trial with up to $100 in credit for new customers
24/7 human support included on all plans, no extra fee
Global datacenter footprint supports low-latency deployment worldwide
cons: Less brand recognition than AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure for enterprise buyers
Configurable pricing can make cost comparison less straightforward than flat-tier competitors
Smaller ecosystem of managed services and marketplace add-ons than hyperscale clouds
Company size (51-200 employees) is modest relative to larger cloud competitors
Support and documentation quality reports vary across independent reviews

faqs.question: What is Kamatera used for?
faqs.answer: Kamatera is a cloud infrastructure provider used to host fully configurable VPS instances, dedicated servers, and applications, billed per minute of actual usage.

faqs.question: How much does Kamatera cost?
faqs.answer: Kamatera's base configurations range from about $4.00 to $57.60 per month, with actual pricing scaling based on the exact CPU, RAM, storage, and bandwidth a customer configures.

faqs.question: When was Kamatera founded?
faqs.answer: Kamatera traces its origins to 1995 as part of the Omnix Computers group, with operations based in Petah Tikva, Israel and New York, United States.

faqs.question: Does Kamatera offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kamatera offers a 30-day free trial with up to $100 in server usage credit on one server.

faqs.question: How many datacenters does Kamatera operate?
faqs.answer: Kamatera operates more than 18 datacenters across North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Kamatera support?
faqs.answer: Kamatera supports both Windows and Linux operating systems on its configurable cloud VPS and dedicated server instances.
